parentQuestion: Which statement about malnutrition-induced night blindness is most accurate?

scaffolds.q: Night blindness = difficulty seeing in dim light (rod dysfunction).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Rods handle dim light. Night blindness means rods aren't working properly.

scaffolds.q: Vitamin A (retinol) is required to make the chromophore in photopigments.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Vitamin A → retinal → 11-cis-retinal (the light-absorbing part of rhodopsin).

scaffolds.q: Vitamin A DEFICIENCY reduces the ability to make rhodopsin.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! No vitamin A = no 11-cis-retinal = no functional rhodopsin.

scaffolds.q: Rods are affected first because night vision is most sensitive to rhodopsin levels.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Rod sensitivity depends on having lots of rhodopsin available.

scaffolds.q: Vitamin D is for calcium/bones, NOT for vision. Vitamin A is for vision.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Don't confuse them! Vitamin A = retinol = retina-related.

scaffolds.q: EARLY vitamin A deficiency is REVERSIBLE with supplements.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Restore vitamin A → restore rhodopsin production → restore night vision.

scaffolds.q: Prolonged deficiency can cause irreversible damage (xerophthalmia, corneal damage).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Early intervention is key - simple night blindness can be reversed.

scaffolds.q: Early vitamin A deficiency night blindness is reversible with supplements.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Restoring vitamin A restores the rhodopsin supply chain.

scaffolds.q: The answer is 'vitamin supplements can reverse degenerative changes' (early stages).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not vitamin D (wrong vitamin), not cones affected more (rods first), not cone dysfunction (night blindness = rod problem). Early supplementation works.
